speaker: interviewer
text: --in Toronto, and I'm interviewing Dennis Urstein, and this is tape two.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, uh, as I was saying before, uh, I-- I was Jewish. And not only that, uh, you know, in Austria, there was always anti-Semitism. And if anybody made any remarks to me, like, dirty Jew, which was very-- you know, [NON-ENGLISH], and I'm not going to spell that because that is really insignificant. But it used to be [NON-ENGLISH] this and [NON-ENGLISH], which means, uh, uh, pigs-- uh, pigs-- uh, pig's Jew-- pig Jew, or something like that, if-- if you can translate it literally. I knocked his brains out, and I had a lot of fights. And nobody-- although my mother always said, don't fight, you know, you're going to get hurt, I-- I didn't take this-- this was-- this was taboo as far as I'm concerned. And, uh but other than that, I got along very well with almost everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you at a public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was. Public-- for four years in a public school, then I went to, uh, what they called the junior high school, which was also public. You know, there were two divisions. You either went to the gymnasium, which was privately owned, and-- and you had to pay for your tuition down there, uh, uh, or you went to the public school system as such, which usually started at six, from the time you were six years old till you were 14. That's it, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there many Jewish kids at your school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that I would say it's about, oh, 20%, 25%. Uh, there were a lot of Jewish kids down there. It was an integrated area. Uh, see, the, uh-- the, uh-- the area we lived in, which was the 20th district, was adjacent to the 2nd district. And the 2nd district was 90% Jewish. That's where Jews-- most of the Jews lived down there. And, uh, so, uh, childhood was, uh-- it was beautiful.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your address?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Streffleurgasse funf. S-T-R-E-F-F-L-E-U-R-G-A-S-S-E. 20th district, number five.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how large was your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, uh, a kitchen, and a big bedroom, living room, that was it. And, you know, sometimes when you-- as we live, uh, today, even my sister and myself, we usually talk about it. I said, how the hell we got in there with all four of us is beyond me. It's just-- there was a double bed for my parents. And there were two beds, one that was a chaise lounge where my sister slept, and I slept, uh, on a bed that, uh-- that you put up at night and you close it again during the day. That was it. And my mother cooked, and she washed. And she did everything. And today, you know, what women need-- they need two washing machines and four dryers. And, uh, they need a maid. And she baked, and she cooked, and she-- she-- she did the sewing. And she was singing, and she was happy. And she worked very, very hard. That poor woman worked. And she was-- if I miss anything, even today, here again-- you always have to hit me below the belt.

speaker: interviewer
text: I miss my parents. Even today, I know they wouldn't be alive, but the reason I miss my parents is-- is, primarily, they didn't see me grow up. I couldn't be a good son to them. I couldn't give them, show them, do for them, what I intended to do. You know, I-- just very briefly, going back, when I sang in that choir in the [GERMAN], and I used to get 25 shillings? I never spent a goddamn penny for myself. It's to buy gifts. My sister always tells me that, like I was nuts. I always bought gifts for either my father, my mother, and my sister. The only money that I spent was that I-- that I made extra. There was extra on Sunday when there was a wedding. And the father of the bride believed that the service and the singing of the choir was beautiful, he gave some money to them. It was split up on everybody, and then we went into the-- we had a, uh-- uh, uh, an automat, you know the buffets down there? And we ate. We blew it all. It was lovely. And I started smoking when I was 13 years old, and, you know, the whole bit. Were you a member of any Jewish organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I-- I-- I was. The reason I joined them was because of the girls. I liked-- I always loved girls. I still love girls. One was the [? Betah, ?] and the other one was, uh, [NON-ENGLISH]. Oh, I'm sorry. It was the other one.

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, something like that. Yeah. But I was there very briefly, uh, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father believe in Zionism?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No. No, I don't think so. I don't believe so. No. No. Mm, I-- I think that-- I'm going by recollections, you know, and, uh, after all, I was a kid. I was a child. Um, I didn't know about Zionism, anyhow. I knew there was a Palestine. Very, very vaguely did I know there was a Palestine. But that's about all. That's all I knew. I-- I-- I knew the expression next year in Jerusalem on the Passover, that I knew, obviously. But it didn't have any meaning for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: But, uh, to sing in-- in a synagogue, in the temple, OK, did a great deal for me because I got feelings. Uh, what do they say here? They have an expression here, uh, that a guy says, uh, uh, he's a cardiac Jew. In other words, in his heart he's a Jew, but he doesn't do anything else. Uh, you know, if-- I'm not putting myself into-- into the-- into-- into this group, as such, but-- but I was too young. You know, today, as you know, and as everybody else knows, uh, kids have a chance for a Jewish education. I'm sure that there-- there must have been something down there too at that time, and maybe there was. But I didn't know about it. How do you feel about your Jewishness now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, uh, maybe even stronger than ever because if you-- if-- if you burn yourself, or if you get a traffic ticket, you remember when and how you burned yourself, and you remember where you got your traffic ticket. And that's it. And if I remember that what I saw, that happened-- and I never use the expression what happened to me. It happened to all of us. Uh, if that doesn't teach, or taught, me a lesson, then what the hell will? Simple as that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you a member of a synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yes, yeah. My mother-in-law got me in there about, what, 25, 30 years ago. Habonim, the builders. I only go there twice a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it's mostly survivors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, no, no, no, no, no. It used to be. Uh, as-- as a matter of fact, it wasn't-- it wasn't survivors that started that synagogue. I think it was, uh, immigrants that started it that came here either during the war from Portugal-- there were-- you know, there were people that came during the war from Portugal. Most of them were Germans and Austrians. And, uh, it-- it blossomed. And today you have a mixture of, uh, new Canadians and very old Canadian, but young people, mostly. So, you know, the old ones are dying out. Most of them are dead anyhow. There are just a few around.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to go back to your grandfather. Do you know what his occupation was?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a printer. My father wrote articles for-- for-- for some of the, uh, social democratic newspapers, always, uh, anti-Nazi. He used to have fights with-- he used to belong to a group of people that were-- you know, the other day we were talking about. In German-- or in Austrian-- it was called a [? Schutzbund ?]. But I think that if you translate it, it was called marshals, you know, that-- that took care of their own. There was a demonstration or there was something like that, these were the people that protected the people that were demonstrating, or something like that. And, uh, they always had fights. They used to fight with the Nazis. They used to fight with, uh, what we used to call the, uh-- the [? schwarzen ?] the black ones, which was the conservatives. Uh, and, uh, I remember one time, when he came home and his-- he always wore white shirts. This is always in my mind. And, uh, it was full of blood. And my mother was carrying on and he says, just wash it. That's it. That's-- that's the whole thing. And also I remember another incident that I-- that I didn't talk to you about, and that was in Vienna there is something like in, uh-- in-- in-- in Scandinavia and Denmark, like Tivoli Garden. It's called a [NON-ENGLISH]. There is the ferris wheel, which is usually in pictures when you look at Vienna, and you know, you see the church and you see a ferris wheel. Well, that was an amusement park. It was fabulous. And, you know, this was the outing for us. And we used to take the streetcar and just go down there, and the streetcar back.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, uh, I remember one incident where my father was very-- well, not too short, but I think that my father would be about 5' 7' or something like that, 5' 6', 5' 7'. But he was really well endowed, strictly Viennese-- he had a big stomach. But he was as strong and agile as they come, OK? And then we went home one night. It was about 8:00, and we went home by streetcar. And there was a guy down there who said something about [GERMAN] or something like that, and you know something? He threw him out of the streetcar. I'll never forget. My mother-- you know, my mother was the Jewish mother. She was scared. But not him. He was a real tough character. Do you know the names of any of the publications your father worked for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The, uh-- the the That's about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe yourself as a kid?

speaker: interviewee
text: How would I describe myself about what?

speaker: interviewer
text: As a kid.

speaker: interviewee
text: As a kid. I think just a normal child. We had a lot of love at home. You know, that is something that I-- I am extremely happy we instilled in our daughter. We were huggers and kissers. At-- not at special occasions, always. I would never go to bed without kissing my father and my mother goodbye. I would never get up in the morning without kissing, and in between there, when I caught them, I kissed them too.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, I-- I also was-- I wouldn't call it obnoxious. In-- In Austrian, you say [? ich bin ?] [? schlimmer ?] [? bub. ?] I was, uh, not a bad boy. What do you call it? Schlimm-- schlimm is called naughty. That's it. OK. And, uh, when I did something or said something and my father or my mother used to say, don't say that, usually a kid would take notice of that and don't say it. Action. Me, said it again. So I got second notice. Don't say it. If I said it again, I got hit over the head, which I deserved.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, when you say mother, my sister tells me that story because I-- I nearly forgotten it. My mother had a lot of patience with me. I was her son. But there was a time when she lost her temper. And when I saw that she lost her temper, and she started chasing me, I went under the bed-- the double bed-- so they couldn't get me. So they-- they both took two brooms, broomsticks. One was poking me from one side. One was poking-- so I had to get out of there somewhere. I did get out. And then my mother gave me a couple of hits.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you know something? I started to cry. And my sister tells me, five minutes later, I made a [INAUDIBLE] to mother and I kissed her. That's me. That's me all over. Do you know if you had any particular ambition?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, as a matter of fact, yes. I don't know why. I wanted to become an engineer. Don't ask me why. I wanted to become an engineer. And I think, you know, there is a why. I had a friend whose father was an engineer, and who told me that when he grows up-- I think he was 12 years old or 13 years old-- that he's going to go to the [NON-ENGLISH] to become an engineer in Vienna. So I wanted to become an engineer, but it didn't-- it had no meaning whatsoever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have many friends who were not Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I'd say so, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And as the Nazis came to power in Germany and that spilled over, how did that affect your relationship with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS]. Well, first of all, nobody talked to me. I mean, I was persona non grata, uh, with the exception of-- I do recall this-- this-- this Rudy Kamfierer who went to school with me, uh, uh, who-- who was, uh, known to be an-- an illegal Nazi, as they called him, because the Nazi party wasn't allowed to exist, uh, between 1933 and-- 1934 and 1938. The government disallowed all the parties. Just one party. OK. And, uh, used to wear those white stockings and-- and the leiderhosen, you know, the leather pants. And that always, uh, uh, showed people who was a Nazi. And that was Rudy Kamfierer. Rudy Kamfierer used to go with me-- I remember that incident. Uh, in our building, in our house, you know, there was-- there were a couple of businesses. There was a dry cleaning and there was a-- a green grocer. And the green grocer was-- I-- I can only say right now was a nebbish. Uh, he-- he was. He wasn't stupid or something like that, but he had-- he was always walking bent forward, and he was very frail. And we, as kids, used to make fun of him, everybody. And I do recall that Rudy Kamfierer said to me, with his hand on my shoulder-- this was I think a week or maybe even four, five days be-- before Hitler marched into Austria-- he said, [GERMAN] In other words, what I translated he said, Dolphy, you don't have to worry about a thing. Nothing's going to happen to you. But that son of a bitch down there, we're going to fix him or kill him. And it didn't say anything to me. I remembered this incident after the war when I started to look for Mr. Rudy Kamfierer. I didn't find him. He got killed, uh, I think in Hungary or in Russia or something. And he was an SS man. But his father and mother lived down there, and I think his brother, when I came back. But, uh, so that was the attitude. And as far as that was concerned, uh, uh--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did-- how did the Anschluss affect your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We had a family in our building, the [? Popeks. ?] The guy went to school with me, same age. And, uh, they came from something called [? Retania ?] or something. I don't know where the heck. This is somewhere in Poland or in northern Russia, but somewhere near Poland. She was an-- if I may use this word-- an old ugly prostitute. But she had a son. Who knows. And she lived with a guy down there. The guy she lived with was a very swarthy character, you know, very dark, almost black, also from that area. He never did anything. He was quiet. He didn't say anything. Maybe because she cooked for him and cleaned for him. He didn't say-- he lived there with her. But she was a real anti-Semite. She hated Jews like there was no tomorrow. But at the time, she couldn't do anything. But when Hitler came in, she, uh, apparently went to the authorities, uh, about my father. [SIGHS] Ah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what happened was that, uh, apparently he, uh-- I was arrested first, before he was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: In hiding. But he was arrested too, but he was let-- you see, that-- that-- that-- there's something that-- one should never put a label on people, but sometimes, it just happens. Uh, the Viennese are a very funny, funny people. Uh, there were a lot of Nazis that weren't Nazis. They were Nazis. Maybe they were afraid, or maybe they-- they saw some gain by becoming Nazis. My father knew a lot of people. And he knew a lot of people in the police department. And I think that due to the fact-- I think, I'm not sure-- that due to the fact of this what one would call connection, he was later on released and came home. Uh, he was, uh, incarcerated in a place called [NON-ENGLISH], which means the main court building, in-- in-- in-- I think it's the 9th district that this was in. And, uh, I was picked up and, uh, you know, the-- the-- the-- the period of-- [SIGHS]. First of all, I found out later that most of the people that were arrested in March and were sent to a concentration camp were sent to Dachau. This I found out later. How the hell I ended up in Buchenwald, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in 1938?

speaker: interviewee
text: 19-- that's right. That was in 1938. And, uh, uh--

speaker: interviewer
text: So how old were you?

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember much about this period?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's-- that's-- that's the thing that-- and you know what? I have-- I thought about it again this morning, OK? When-- when-- when somebody throws me a candy, I-- I start chewing it and biting it until I get to the inside of the-- the soft core. And then I said to myself, stop. Don't. If you don't remember, don't think about it. I don't want to, because what the hell? Why should I conjure this-- this memory back if I don't-- if I-- you know, I-- I told you the last time that I had, uh-- I had two nervous breakdowns. I had shock treatment twice, once in England, and once in Canada. And if-- if because of that, I have forgotten a big part-- it's not that big a part. It's only six or eight months or whatever, seven months. Then, uh, I'm very happy I did. And-- and-- and it's like a blur. I do remember being in that school, or being, remember, uh, uh, with a lot of people that are much older. I don't think-- I don't even remember if there were some boys of my age down there. I don't recall this anymore. I know exactly where it was because I visited. I went back again.

speaker: interviewer
text: To Buchenwald?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. I went back again to that school in Vienna where-- where we were-- where we were held. You know, it's like a holding pen. And, uh, I never went back to Buchenwald. Except I was there for one day, but, uh, you know, that was in 1944 and this-- when we were, uh, replaced, going from-- one from Auschwitz to other places until we ended up in [INAUDIBLE] and then in Dachau. Uh, but-- but this-- this-- this time period, it's like-- it's like it's not there. It's there and it's not there. And I'm not going to-- if, with your permission, I'm not going to talk about it because I don't want to remember it. I have enough to remember that bothers me.  Sometimes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, do you know how you were released from Buchenwald?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I signed a document, that I remember, not to, uh, uh, discuss my being there. And Adolf Urstein, and that's it. And then all of a sudden, I came home. And, uh, it, uh-- it was what? September, yeah, September I came home. That's right, 1938. And there was a lot of crying, and, uh, a lot of kissing and a lot of hugging and, uh--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your parents making any attempts at this time to get out of Austria?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, no. No. No. No. My-- my-- my dad, like so many others, many, many others, thousands of them, all over the place, all over Europe, especially, I would say in-- in Austria, because in Germany they knew already. And they still did-- they still stayed there because what the heck? If when Hitler came to Germany in 1933 and the Nuremberg Laws came out a few days-- a few years later, why did no people leave? They could leave at that. There was no war. They could leave, so they left. They had to leave everything, but leave. No, they didn't. Well, I'm not going to blame them because nobody could in his wildest dream imagine that something like this annihilation of a-- of a-- of a whole race, uh, would happen. Anyhow, so, no he did not. But when Kristallnacht came, I think that's when he woke up. That was November 1938, when they start this. First they-- they burned down the synagogue, I think, the [NON-ENGLISH], in the [NON-ENGLISH], and all the other synagogues. And then people were put out on the, uh-- on the street, cleaning the streets with brushes, with toothbrushes, and spit upon, and hit upon and [GERMAN] this and [GERMAN] that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- because I was-- I was a-- an idiot. I mean, my mother, she was shaking in her boots. She didn't want anybody to go out. I went out. So, I mean, so I didn't know better. Nothing happened to me, thanks god. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: What went on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Thanks god. See, you remember what I said just now?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said thanks god. How do you like that? Bingo.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened? What was going on in your mind when you saw this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. I just-- I-- I just couldn't understand it. I couldn't understand why-- why-- why it was done. I didn't-- if-- if-- if this is an excuse, I use it as such. I say I was too young. If-- if-- if you compare a 14-year-old today and a 14-year-old then, I think that a 14-year-old today is 50 times as sophisticated because of television and because of education, who knows what, than-- maybe he is. I'm just guessing, but I think that kids today, uh, are more knowledgeable.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now did your sister, at this time, try to get out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, I don't know if she tried or not. But she did. She-- she-- I think she-- she left in 1938 or in '39. I forgot which year it was. She had some friends, and through these friends she received a visa to go to England. Uh, before that, before 1938, my sister attended the conservatory in Vienna. She was one hell of a-- we had a piano at home, and she taught me the piano. And I spit on her if she taught me the piano. Then my father gave me a hit, and I didn't want to play the piano. Then incidentally, he said, you have to take up the violin. I didn't want the violin. It's always a Jewish kid has to learn an instrument.

speaker: interviewer
text: So on what basis--

speaker: interviewee
text: And so she left.

speaker: interviewer
text: On what basis did she get in to England?

speaker: interviewee
text: She had-- as a domestic. And she had a really rough time. But you know, there's something you brought up. If-- if you asked me-- well, I do remember. Usually I don't. If you ask me this lady's name here, that I met just an hour ago, usually, I forget. At this time, I didn't forget. I know what her name is, and I'm very happy to say that I know. But usually, I forget things. The address where my sister worked in England was imprinted in my mind all through the war years. That's how I found her after the war. Today, I haven't got a clue. I don't know a name. I don't know a location. I don't know anything. I knew the name of her employees. I knew the address of her employees. I knew that it was somewhere in Wiltshire, I think, in England. And that's how I found her again. And I somehow got a letter to her after the war. But not today. Today, I don't remember it anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now what did-- I'm gonna--

speaker: interviewee
text: So she worked there--

speaker: interviewer
text: --push you a bit forward, because we want to move ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, go ahead. Shoot, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I want to go over the next period of time. Where did your family go after your sister left?

speaker: interviewee
text: So, well, we, uh-- we-- we went, uh, to, uh-- to Belgium, you know? And, uh, I think we crossed the border in late 1938. And, uh, we were arrested first, and then we were sent to [INAUDIBLE] to Antewerp, where the Jewish community, uh-- oh, you just reminded me of something that I'm going to bring up if it's the last thing I do. You know, you said something to me a little while ago. And you said, Jewishness, how do you feel? Well, let me tell you something. Two incidents, very quickly. When I was liberated in Dachau, I could speak Flemish. Since I knew that my parents' last domicile was in Brussels-- that address I remember-- Rue de la [INAUDIBLE] I remember that like today. It was near the Gare du Nord. That's where they lived. And I wanted to go back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the officer down there in charge of rep-- repatriation asked me who I was, I told him I was a Belgian. OK? So I went in the Belgian transport, and I arrived back. So I arrive on the Gare du Nord, talk about Jewishness. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's interesting. I have a pair of striped pants, a striped shirt. My hair just started to grow a little bit because I don't think we had a haircut for about a month or a month and a half. And I have this cap that I'm going to show you later on that I brought from Dachau, from that guy that we-- that we executed with the word Dachau on there. And that's how I travel. All right? Now this is, uh, May. And we get off the train, and a guy walks up to me, and he says to me in Flemish, are you Jewish? He-- how did he say? [FLEMISH]. I nearly punched him in the face, but then, you know, this guy was a little fellow, and I said, yes. Yeah. He said, uh-huh. OK. He takes out a piece of paper. I'm serious right now. He says, OK. This is where you go first, to get some clothes. He rips it off gives it to me. He said, this is where you're going to go to get some food. And he rips it off and gives it to me. And he said-- and he puts his hand in his pockets and he says, and here's 20 francs so that you can take the streetcar. I said, who are you? He said, I'm with the whoever the Jewish organization was that took care of people that returned. That's the number one thing, because you-- you asked me how I feel. When we arrived in Canada on the first of February, 1951, we sat on the Union Station because we had to wait for a train. We could have taken a streetcar, but we didn't know any better. And we waited, and a guy walks up to me and he says to me, he says, you just arrive here? Yeah. You Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is in English. Now I said yes. And I didn't get mad to hit him because I could see that he was Jewish too. He said, look, I'm from the Jewish Congress Beverly Street, bah bah bah bah bah. If you ever need anything right there-- if you need a loan or something like that, or you need some food, like that-- takes it off, gives it to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me what other people in the world act like that? You tell me and I'll tell you that it isn't so. It's-- it's-- it's-- this is the greatest feeling in the world. It didn't make me religious, but it made me one thing. I'm-- I'm-- I'm as nationalistic about who I am and what I am, and I want to instill this in my daughter and in my granddaughter. And, uh--
